name: St. Moritz Gourmet Festival
tagline: Ten of the world's great chefs descend on the Engadin's grand hotels for a week of kitchen parties, gourmet safaris and dinners at 1,800 metres.
dates: 25–30 August 2026 (confirmed — the festival has moved from its traditional late-January slot to late summer as of 2026); a late-August 2027 edition (expected)
history: The St. Moritz Gourmet Festival was founded in 1994 by local chef Reto Mathis and a circle of St. Moritz hoteliers who had a simple, audacious idea: fly the world's most exciting chefs into the Alps and set them loose in the kitchens of the resort's five-star palace hotels. Billed ever since as 'The Original', it was one of Europe's first destination food festivals, and the formula — each visiting star cooks side-by-side with the executive chef of a partner hotel — remains its signature. Since 1994 more than 250 guest chefs from roughly 39 countries have cooked at the festival, from emerging talents to holders of three Michelin stars.

Over three decades the festival grew into one of the largest events of its kind in Europe, a five-to-six-day marathon of some thirty-plus events drawing several thousand food-obsessed visitors to the Upper Engadin each year. Its beloved formats became institutions in their own right: the Gourmet Safari, which shuttles diners between hotels for a different course (and a different star chef) at every stop; raucous late-night Kitchen Parties where guests eat standing amid the stoves; and intimate four-hands dinners. Themed editions — such as the year the whole festival was dedicated to Japanese cuisine — produced some of its most memorable moments. The festival is widely credited with transforming the region's gastronomy: the Engadin now counts over 200 GaultMillau points and eight Michelin stars, a density unimaginable when the festival began.

In 2026 the festival made its boldest move yet, leaving its historic late-January/early-February slot (where it famously paired with the White Turf horse races on the frozen lake) and repositioning itself in late summer. The first summer edition runs 25–30 August 2026 with ten international guest chefs — among them Kai Ho of Taipei's Taïrroir, Emmanuel Pilon of Le Louis XV–Alain Ducasse in Monaco and Mikael Svensson of Oslo's Kontrast — hosted across nine partner hotels including Badrutt's Palace, the Kulm and Suvretta House. New formats lean into the landscape, most strikingly 'Culinary Moments in Motion': a four-course lunch served aboard a nostalgic dining car rolling along the UNESCO World Heritage Albula railway line.
whyGo: Nowhere else can you eat a different Michelin-calibre chef's food at every course while being chauffeured between belle-époque palace hotels in an Alpine valley. The move to late August adds golden larch-fringed hiking weather, open mountain railways and softer hotel rates to the equation — and the dining-car lunch on the UNESCO Albula line may be the single most scenic meal in Switzerland.

gettingThere.route: Zürich HB → Chur (IC3) → St. Moritz (Rhaetian Railway over the UNESCO Albula line)
gettingThere.cost: CHF 80–160 return 2nd class; supersaver fares from ~CHF 39 each way
gettingThere.notes: The Chur–St. Moritz leg is the UNESCO World Heritage Albula line — sit on the right side leaving Chur for the viaducts. Book SBB supersaver tickets ~2–3 months out; a Half-Fare Card pays for itself on this route alone.

gettingThere.route: Zürich HB → Landquart → Klosters → Vereina tunnel → St. Moritz (via Engadin valley)
gettingThere.cost: Similar to the Albula routing, CHF 80–160 return
gettingThere.notes: Alternative Rhaetian Railway routing through the Vereina tunnel and the Lower Engadin — handy if you want to loop one way via Albula and return this way for two different panoramas.

gettingThere.route: Zürich → A3/A13 to Chur → Julier Pass → St. Moritz (~200 km)
gettingThere.cost: ~CHF 60–90 fuel return + CHF 40 motorway vignette if not already held
gettingThere.notes: The Julier Pass is fully open and beautiful in late August. Parking in St. Moritz is scarce and pricey (hotel valet or Serletta garage); honestly, the train is the better experience here.

costs.notes: Event tickets are the variable: single dinners and lunches run roughly CHF 200–450, gourmet safaris and gala finales more. Thrifty means a guesthouse in Celerina or Pontresina (10 min away), supersaver trains and one or two events; midrange a 4-star in St. Moritz with two signature dinners; splurge is 3 nights in a partner palace hotel (Badrutt's, Kulm, Suvretta House) plus safari, kitchen party and the dining-car lunch. Late-summer hotel rates are notably gentler than the old February peak.

weather: Late August at 1,822 m: pleasant days around 15–20°C, crisp nights that can dip toward 5°C, with a chance of short afternoon thunderstorms. Pack layers — smart-casual for daytime events, a jacket or blazer plus something warm for post-dinner walks; the gala evenings skew elegant.
tips: Ticket sales open in mid-June (18 June for the 2026 edition) and the marquee formats — Gourmet Safari, Kitchen Party, the Albula dining-car lunch — sell out within days. Set a reminder and book the moment sales open.

Staying in one of the nine partner hotels puts the festival on your doorstep and often eases access to that hotel's guest-chef dinner; if the palace rates sting, Celerina and Pontresina are 10 minutes away by hourly train.
Mix price points: the lunches, apéros and opening events cost far less than the gala dinners but feature the same chefs — a smart way to sample three chefs for the price of one dinner.

Note the calendar change: from 2026 the festival is a late-summer event, so it no longer coincides with White Turf (February). Pair it instead with a Bernina Express day to Poschiavo or a Muottas Muragl sunset hike.
Dress code is Alpine-elegant: jacket for men at the gala dinners, no one bats an eye at good sneakers by day. Swiss francs on a card are fine everywhere; tipping beyond rounding up is not expected.
Buy SBB supersaver fares and consider a Half-Fare Card (CHF 120/year) if this is one of several Swiss trips from your Zürich base — it halves this route and every other.

localRecs.name: Hatecke
localRecs.note: Cult Engadin butcher-deli on Via Maistra — air-dried Bündnerfleisch and salsiz sliced to order; the sleek little eatery does superb open sandwiches for a light lunch between festival events.

localRecs.name: Hanselmann's
localRecs.note: St. Moritz's grand 19th-century konditorei with a painted sgraffito façade — come for Engadiner Nusstorte (caramel-walnut tart) and hot chocolate.

localRecs.name: Devil's Place, Hotel Waldhaus am See
localRecs.note: Guinness-record whisky bar with over 2,500 bottles and a lakeside terrace — the ideal nightcap after a kitchen party.

localRecs.name: Muottas Muragl
localRecs.note: Century-old funicular above Samedan to the definitive Upper Engadin panorama — lakes strung down the valley like beads. Go for sunset; the summit restaurant is genuinely good.

localRecs.name: Lej da Staz
localRecs.note: A 40-minute forest walk from St. Moritz Dorf to a warm-ish moorland swimming lake with a lovely rustic restaurant — the locals' antidote to the glitz.

photos.caption: Badrutt's Palace Hotel, the festival's most storied partner venue, in late-summer light above Lake St. Moritz
The Kulm Hotel St. Moritz, birthplace of Alpine winter tourism and a Gourmet Festival stalwart since 1994
The Upper Engadin lake plateau from Muottas Muragl — the backdrop to every course of the festival
